retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Coagulopathies -- Etiology and Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hemostasis is a complex biological process that involves a delicate balance between procoagulant and anticoagulant forces. Conditions that disrupt this balance can lead to coagulopathies, characterized by either excessive bleeding or thrombotic events. Generally, reduced procoagulant factors or impaired fibrinolysis, thrombocytopenia, or impaired platelet function increase the bleeding risk. Inherited coagulopathies with bleeding tendency most commonly arise from genetic mutations affecting clotting factor production or function, such as hemophilia A and hemophilia B, or von Willebrand disease, and rarely affect platelet function. [8] [9] Von Willebrand disease is the most common, affecting up to 1% of the general population. [10] Platelet function disorders, such as Bernard-Soulier syndrome and Glanzmann thrombasthenia, are rare but may be underestimated. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypercoagulability -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercoagulability disorders are either acquired or inherited. [7] However, actual thrombosis occurs due to the interplay of both genetic and environmental factors and follows the multiple hit hypothesis, [8] [9] [10] thus explaining the inter-individual differences observed in patients with inherited mutations. [11] Genetic factors can now be identified in up to 30% of patients with VTE and are mainly attributable to factor V Leiden and prothrombin G2021A mutation. These two thrombophilias implicate a weak thrombotic risk. Other inherited thrombophilias are rare such as antithrombin III, protein C and protein S deficiency (around 1% in the general population) but pose a higher risk for thrombosis. Acquired factors also influence the coagulation cascade and include surgery, pregnancy, hormonal replacement therapy, contraception, malignancy, inflammation, infection, and heparin-induced thrombocytopenia. [12] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Clotting Mechanism -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercoagulability (thrombophilia) is any disorder of the blood that predisposes a person to thrombosis. This may be a result of inherited clotting disorders such as a Factor V Leiden mutation or an acquired clotting disorder such as disseminated intravascular coagulation.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Coagulation Pathways -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Generally, factor deficiencies are mostly autosomal recessive, except for factors VIII and IX, which are X-linked recessive. In contrast, anticoagulant deficiencies, such as factors Z, C, S, and antithrombin III, are primarily autosomal dominant.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Coagulopathies -- Etiology and Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: On the other side of the scale, thromboembolic diseases such as arterial thromboembolism, including myocardial infarction and stroke, and venous thromboembolism account for at least 1 in 4 deaths worldwide. Hypercoagulability may arise from multiple inherited and acquired factors associated with increased procoagulant factors or decreased anticoagulant or fibrinolytic factors. Venous thromboembolism affects 1 to 3 per 1000 adults and is one of the main mortality causes worldwide. [19] The inherited thrombophilias, such as natural anticoagulant deficiencies or gain-of-function mutations, including G20210AFII, increase venous thrombotic risk. The most common inherited thrombophilia is factor V Leiden. Acquired predisposing factors for venous thromboembolism include age, cancer, pregnancy, obesity, trauma, hospitalizations, and surgeries, among others. [20] [21] Myeloproliferative diseases and paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria increase the risk for unusual site thrombosis. [22] Other rare immune-based hematological diseases, such as thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura and heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, may present with severe thrombocytopenia and paradoxical thrombosis. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Factor XIII Deficiency -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: All bleeding disorders, including factor deficiencies and platelet dysfunction syndromes, can mimic FXIII deficiency. The following list outlines potential differential diagnoses: Inherited afibrinogenemia or dyshypofibrinogenemias, α 2 -plasmin inhibitor deficiency, plasminogen activator inhibitor-1 deficiency, hemophilia A or B, and type III von Willebrand disease. Platelet dysfunction disorders such as Glanzmann thrombasthenia, Bernard Souilier syndrome, and von Willebrand disease types I and II. Additional coagulation factor deficiencies, including FII, FV, FVII, FX, and FXI. Acquired causes of FXIII deficiency, including malignancy, autoimmune disorders, medications, hyperconsumption, disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), and liver disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercoaguability testing may include checking the amount of proteins C and S, antithrombin III, or mutational testing for factor V Leiden or prothrombin gene mutation. Acquired thrombophilia testing may also be indicated, such as antiphospholipid antibody testing, which includes lupus anticoagulant, IgG/IgM anticardiolipin, and anti-beta-2-glycoprotein I.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Coagulopathies -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Inherited and acquired thrombophilia testing includes screening for natural anticoagulant deficiencies, such as protein C, protein S,  and antithrombin factor; activated protein C resistance; factor  V Leiden; and FII G20210A mutations. Testing for antiphospholipid syndrome is reserved for patients with unprovoked venous thromboembolism, unexplained arterial thrombosis, obstetric complications, and other clinical criteria of microvascular thrombosis. [119] Quantitative disorders of FVIII, FIX, FXI, and FXII, and mutations in fibrinogen genes ( FGG , FGA , FGB ), PAI-1 (4G/5G), and the protective gene of factor XIII (Val24Leu) are not currently indicated for investigation. In addition, polymorphisms of the MTHFR gene (677C→T, 1298A→C) are not associated with thrombosis and are therefore not recommended for inclusion in the panel. Previous guidelines proposed homocysteine testing, but recent changes suggest this is not a strong risk factor. [128] [129]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Clotting Factors -- Pathophysiology -- Hemophilias
retrieved_docs.content: Hemophilia B, sometimes called Christmas disease, is an X-linked recessive coagulopathy resulting in dysfunction of IX. As with hemophilia A, hemophilia B also cause a prolonged PTT. The difference is hemophilia A is a cofactor deficiency while hemophilia B is a protease deficiency; therefore, desmopressin not be a good treatment option as these patients require recombinant factor IX. Hemophilia B present with the same symptoms as hemophilia A. It is important to note that hemophilia A and B have a normal prothrombin/INR.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypercoagulability -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Prothrombin is the precursor of thrombin, which is factor II. The prothrombin G20210A mutation is the second most common inherited risk factor for thrombosis and leads to increased levels of prothrombin which demonstrates a higher risk for arterial and venous thrombotic events. [12] [11] [20] It is due to a single point mutation. It is seen commonly in Caucasians.

retrieved_docs.title: Factor V Deficiency -- Differential Diagnosis -- Platelet Dysfunction
retrieved_docs.content: Von Willebrand disease, the most common inherited bleeding disorder, occurs with a variable clinical spectrum ranging from mild mucosal bleeding in type 1 to severe hemorrhage resembling hemophilia in type 3. Laboratory findings include prolonged or normal aPTT, normal prothrombin time, normal-to-low platelet count, and normal-to-low factor VIII level. Less common inherited platelet function disorders include Glanzmann thrombasthenia (αIIbβ3 integrin deficiency or defect) and Bernard–Soulier syndrome (GPIb-IX-V complex deficiency or defect). These disorders show normal prothrombin time and aPTT, reflecting impaired platelet adhesion or aggregation independent of the coagulation cascade. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Clotting Mechanism -- Pathophysiology -- Disorders of Secondary Hemostasis: Clotting Factor Defects
retrieved_docs.content: Factor V Leiden Vitamin K deficiency Hemophilia Anti-phospholipid antibody syndrome Disseminated intravascular coagulation Liver disease Medication-induced

retrieved_docs.title: Bleeding Disorders -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bleeding disorders fall into two main categories: inherited and acquired.  Inherited bleeding disorders have a genetic predisposition and involve a deficiency of coagulation factors. Acquired bleeding disorders can be caused by conditions that an individual may develop at any point during their lifetime.  These can be broader in range and dependent on comorbid conditions.  The focus of this discussion will be on congenital coagulopathies; acquired bleeding disorders will be considered to be outside the scope of the information presented here.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Hemostasis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: General Principle. The Virchow’s triad of hypercoagulability, vascular stasis, and vascular trauma, described in 1856, remains a true predictor of thrombosis. Hypercoagulability Stasis Trauma Etiologies. The physiology of coagulation undergoes alteration due to various factors, including: Genetics Medications Procoagulant Anticoagulation defects of the coagulation cascade Quantitative defects of the integral components of the coagulation Qualitative defects of the integral components of coagulation. Clinical Presentations . With the altering of hemostatic physiology, various clinical outcomes including: Pulmonary embolism Deep vein thrombosis Stroke Myocardial infarction Coagulopathies. Few of the disorders of coagulation include: Anti-thrombin 3 deficiency, Protein C deficiency, Hyperhomocysteinemia, Anti-phospholipid antibody syndrome Risk Factors. Some acquired factors influencing the coagulation include [10] : Pregnancy Trauma Malignancy-related hypercoagulable state

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Clotting Mechanism -- Pathophysiology -- Disorders of Primary Hemostasis: vWF, Platelet defects, or Receptor Interference
retrieved_docs.content: Von Willebrand Factor disease Bernard-Soulier disease Glanzmann thrombasthenia Medication-induced

retrieved_docs.title: Hypercoagulability -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: To diagnose hypercoagulability syndromes, the combination of screening tests, confirmation tests, and risk factors all must merit consideration. [19] The thrombophilia screen includes functional assays for antithrombin III, protein c and s deficiency, PCR for factor V Leiden mutation and prothrombin G2021A mutation, testing for antiphospholipid antibodies and homocysteine level. [12] Additional laboratory investigations include routine coagulation panel, d-dimer, [62] and CBC.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1751
retrieved_docs.content: Factor VIII may be used to treat coagulation and hemorrhagic disorders, as it helps correct deficiencies in the clotting cascade that underlie these bleeding conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2058
retrieved_docs.content: Human factor VIII is used as a therapeutic agent to treat coagulation and hemorrhagic disorders, helping to correct deficient clotting activity and control bleeding. By supplementing the missing or insufficient factor VIII, it restores normal hemostasis in conditions such as hemophilia A and other bleeding disorders, thereby reducing the risk of severe hemorrhage and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 24
retrieved_docs.content: class. Collectively, these classifications illustrate the mechanistic relationships among hereditary‑angioedema therapies, heme‑related products, heparin derivatives, protein antithrombins, low‑molecular‑weight heparins, enzyme‑based fibrinolytics, other antithrombotic agents, and hemostatics, as well as their shared adverse‑effect considerations and relevance for patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Kyselina tranexamicová, an antifibrinolytic medication, is contraindicated in patients with abnormalities of the faktory krevní koagulace (blood coagulation factors). Because tranexamic acid can promote clot stability, its use should be avoided in individuals who have deficiencies or disorders of these coagulation factors, as it may increase the risk of thrombotic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1882
retrieved_docs.content: Human factor VIII, also referred to as faktor VIII, is used as a therapeutic agent for coagulation and hemorrhagic disorders. By supplying the deficient clotting protein, factor VIII, human helps restore normal hemostasis and control bleeding in patients affected by these disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1636
retrieved_docs.content: abnormalities (Gestation abnormality) are linked as a downstream condition of bleeding in pregnancy, indicating that hemorrhagic events can contribute to broader developmental disturbances of the pregnancy. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how various placental positions, coagulation abnormalities, timing of hemorrhage, and associated hematologic findings converge under the single clinical concept of bleeding in pregnancy, emphasizing the importance of recognizing each subtype for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and targeted management in obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 605
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors are classified as antithrombotic agents and function through the inhibition of coagulation factor Xa, a key step in the clotting cascade. This class of drugs exerts its therapeutic effect by directly targeting factor Xa activity, thereby reducing thrombus formation. Rivaroxaban is a specific medication that belongs to the Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors class, exemplifying the use of this mechanism in clinical practice for the prevention and treatment of thromboembolic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2694
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 70, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 70 can increase blood viscosity and promote coagulation, its use in individuals who already have a propensity for clot formation may exacerbate the underlying thrombotic condition and increase the risk of serious vascular complications. Clinicians should therefore avoid administering Dextran 70 to patients diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this pro‑thrombotic risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2296
retrieved_docs.content: Antifibrinolytics are a class of medications that inhibit the enzymatic breakdown of fibrin clots, thereby promoting hemostasis in situations of excessive bleeding. Vitamin K 1 (phylloquinone) and vitamin K 2 (menaquinone) are distinct forms of the vitamin K family that serve as essential cofactors for the γ‑carboxylation of several clotting factors, a process required for normal coagulation. Although both antifibrinolytics and the vitamin K compounds influence the coagulation system, antifibrinolytics are not subtypes of vitamin K 1 or vitamin K 2; they act through a different mechanism by directly blocking plasminogen activation rather than by providing the vitamin K‑dependent enzymatic support needed for clot factor maturation. Recognizing this distinction is important for patient care, as antifibrinolytic agents such as tranexamic acid are used to control acute hemorrhage, whereas vitamin K 1 or vitamin K 2 supplementation is employed to correct vitamin K deficiency–related bleeding disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2852
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 40, a low‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in the presence of الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 40 can increase blood viscosity and promote clot formation, its use in patients with thrombotic disorders may exacerbate the underlying condition and raise the risk of further vascular occlusion. Consequently, clinicians should avoid administering Dextran 40 to individuals diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2737
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 75, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease); using dextran 75 in this condition may promote clot formation and worsen clinical outcomes, so clinicians should avoid prescribing it to individuals who have thrombotic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1571
retrieved_docs.content: Antifibrinolytics are agents that inhibit the breakdown of fibrin clots and are biochemically linked to vitamin K compounds, encompassing both vitamin K1 and vitamin K2. Alfa‑2‑antiplasmin is a specific antifibrinolytic protein that functions within this class, contributing to the stabilization of formed clots. The association of antifibrinolytics with both forms of vitamin K underscores their role in the vitamin K–dependent activation of clotting factors, which is essential for normal hemostasis. Recognizing that antifibrinolytic activity can arise from or interact with both vitamin K1 and vitamin K2 informs clinical decisions in managing bleeding disorders, anticoagulant therapy, and related coagulation abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1941
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 70 may prevent the development of venous thrombosis (التخثر الوريدي). Venous thrombosis is considered a child condition within the broader category of venous disorder (اضطراب الوريد). Consequently, the ability of Dextran 70 to inhibit venous thrombosis is relevant for the prevention and management of venous disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1661
retrieved_docs.content: Desmopressin is a hemostatic agent (isa), meaning it is classified among medications that promote clot formation. By increasing plasma levels of factor VIII and von Willebrand factor and enhancing platelet adhesion, desmopressin is used to treat or prevent bleeding in conditions such as mild hemophilia A, von Willebrand disease, and in patients undergoing surgery or on antiplatelet therapy. Its role as a hemostatic drug makes it an important therapeutic option for managing bleeding disorders and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 916
retrieved_docs.content: previa without hemorrhage). Although the latter denotes a previa without active bleeding, it is grouped together with hemorrhage in pregnancy to emphasize its close clinical relationship and potential progression to bleeding. Overall, the knowledge base links inverted uterus and various hemorrhagic conditions to the maternal antenatal period, situates hemorrhage in pregnancy within the spectrum of gestational abnormalities and puerperal disorders, and delineates multiple specific hemorrhagic subtypes—ranging from placental previa variants to coagulation‑related parturient hemorrhages—thereby providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing pregnancy‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 252
retrieved_docs.content: is a subclass of blood‑forming organ drugs and encompasses the more specific group “vitamin K and other hemostatics,” which includes agents that promote clot formation through vitamin K‑dependent pathways. Together, these interrelated classifications illustrate how antithrombotic, hemostatic, and enzymatic agents are organized within the pharmacological landscape of blood and blood‑forming organ therapeutics, providing a structured framework for understanding their mechanisms and clinical applications.
